Facts.text: Betar, the last outpost of Bar Kochba, falls to Rome
Battle of Maserfield: British Anglo-Saxon King Penda of Mercia defeats and kills Oswald of Bernicia
The last major Viking army to raid England, led by King Edward and Earl Æthelred, is defeated at the Battle of Tettenhall by the allied forces of Mercia and Wessex
Henry I is crowned King of England in Westminster Abbey, London
Anti-Jewish riots break out in Arnstadt, Germany
Battle of Otterburn, a border skirmish between the Scottish and the English in Northern England
Castilian sailors fire an attack on the Jewish ghetto of Barcelona, killing hundreds
Battle of Vorskla: Tatars defeat Russians and Lithuanians
Duke John VI of Bavaria visits Dutch mystic Lidwina, known as Christ's bride
French and Turkish troops occupy Nice
Battle of Ponza: Ottoman naval fleet led by Dragut defeats the Genoese fleet under Andrea Doria, capturing seven galleys
Humphrey Gilbert claims Newfoundland for the English crown, establishing the first English colony in North America and the beginning of the British Empire
French troops occupy Stenay during the Franco-Spanish war
On the second day of the St. James' Day Battle (Two Day Battle), the English fleet defeats the Dutch under Michiel de Ruyter
State of Holland obtains Eternal Edict
1,500 Iroquois attack the village of Lachine in New France
During the Jacobite Rising of 1745, Jacobite forces capture a British company of troops under Captain Scott
Pontiac's War: Battle of Bushy Run - British forces led by Henry Bouquet defeat Native American forces at Bushy Run
First Partition of Poland among Austria, Prussia, and Russia is made public
First Spanish ship, San Carlos, enters San Francisco Bay
Battle of Dogger Bank (Fourth Anglo-Dutch War): a British strategic victory, although both sides claim to have won
First ascent of Mount Marcy, which stands at 5,344 feet (1,629 meters) as the highest peak in the Adirondack Mountains of New York
Oregon Territory is divided between the United States and Britain along the 49th parallel
Carl IV of Sweden-Norway is crowned King of Norway in Trondheim
Enlistment in US Union Army increases from 3 months to 2 years
Naval Engagement at Fernandina, Florida, the USS Vincennes ends rebel blockade
President Lincoln signs the first US personal income tax into law, imposing a 3% tax on incomes over $800
US Army abolishes flogging
Union forces prevent Confederate attempts to capture Baton Rouge, Louisiana
Spectrum of a comet is observed for the first time by Giovanni Donati
Japan launches its postal savings system, modeled after a similar system in the United Kingdom
Martial law is imposed in Japan
Standard Oil of New Jersey is established
British and French accord to divide African colonization
World's first traveler's cheques issued by American Express
In Russia, anti-Jewish riots spread from Odessa to other parts of the country
Peter O'Connor of Ireland sets the first officially recognised world long jump record at 24 feet and 11 3/4 inches (7.61 meters) in Dublin, Ireland
Japan's first taxicab service begins in Ginza, Tokyo
Cuba, Uruguay, Mexico, and Argentina proclaim neutrality in WWI
Dutch Cort Van der Linden government issues silver bonds as money
First electric traffic light installed in the US at the intersection of East 105th Street and Euclid Avenue in Cleveland, Ohio
Montenegro declares war against Austria-Hungary in WWI
US and Nicaragua sign treaty granting canal rights to US
Westerschelde leaves due to the German invasion of Belgium
The Latin American Conference convenes in Washington, with representatives from leading South American nations joining the US to discuss conditions in Mexico
Warsaw, evacuated by the Russians, is occupied by the Germans
British troops attack the Ypres Canal in Boesinghe, Belgium
The entire US National Guard is taken into national service, subject to presidential rather than state control
KDKA Pittsburgh presents the first radio broadcast of MLB as the Pirates beat the Phillies 8-0 with Harold Arlin as the first play-by-play broadcaster
Treaty of Berlin: US and Germany sign separate peace treaty
"Little Orphan Annie" comic strip by Harold Gray is first published in the New York Daily News
Plaid Cymru is formed with the aim of disseminating knowledge of the Welsh language, which is in danger of dying out
French and German trade agreement is signed
Cy Williams is the first Phillie to hit for the cycle against the Pittsburgh Pirates
Phillies outfielder Cy Williams hits for the cycle in just four at-bats in a 9-7 victory against the Pirates
Italian athlete Ondina Valla runs a world record time of 11.6 seconds in the semifinals of the 80 m hurdles at the Berlin Olympics; wins the final the next day but can't match the time
Ken Carpenter (50.48 Olympic record) and Gordon Dunn achieve an American 1-2 finish in the men’s discus throw at the Berlin Olympics
St. Louis Browns pitcher John Whitehead no-hits the Detroit Tigers 4-0 in six innings (rain-shortened)
British government cancels Munich Agreement
German troops cross the Russian Kuban River in the Western Caucasus
Sicily: Three US A-36s bomb British headquarters
WWII: At around 11 am during the Battle of Troina, Mount Etna erupts, sending ash and lava miles into the sky
WWII: Soviet forces reclaim Orel and Bjelgorod in the Kharkov region of Ukraine, liberating the cities from German occupation
German forces begin the mass killing of between 40,000 and 50,000 Polish civilians in the Wola district of Warsaw during the uprising
US 79th and 90th Divisions occupy Laval (Mayenne), southwest of Paris
US troops occupy Vannes, Brittany
The Netherlands ceases political action in Indonesia due to UN pressure
Americans Sammy Lee and Bruce Harlan go 1-2 in the 10 m platform diving at the London Olympics
Cleveland Indians set a club record for most double plays in a game (6) in a 3-0 win over Washington Senators at Cleveland Stadium
Earl Shaffer becomes the first person to thru-hike the Appalachian Trail after hiking for 124 days
Jamaica achieves a 1-2 finish in the men's 400 m at the London Olympics, with Arthur Wint (46.2) defeating teammate Herb McKenley for the gold medal
Swedish sweep the medals in 3000 m steeplechase at the London Olympics with Tore Sjöstrand taking gold ahead of teammates Erik Elmsäter and Göte Hagström
A 6.4 earthquake strikes Tungurahua Province, Ecuador, killing 5,000 people
Uprising by Moluccan soldiers from Indonesia at Ambonese Camp, Netherlands
Operation "Big Switch"; Korean War prisoners exchanged at Panmunjom
Belgian race car driver André Milhoux (27) competes in his only Grand Prix, driving a Gordini T32 in Germany; he is forced to retire after 15 laps due to an engine problem
KUAM TV channel 8 in Agana, Guam (CBS/ABC/NBC) begins broadcasting
WCYB TV channel 5 in Bristol-Kingsport, Virginia (NBC) begins broadcasting
"American Bandstand" premieres on network TV (ABC)
Comic strip "Andy Capp" makes its debut
WJZ-TV in Baltimore, MD, begins television broadcasting
42.4 cm of rainfall in Decatur Co, Iowa (state record, until 1998)
Chicago Cardinals (NFL) beat Toronto Argonauts (CFL) 55-26 in a pre-season exhibition at CNE Stadium in Toronto
Burkina Faso (formerly Upper Volta) declares independence from France
118°F (48°C) is recorded at Ice Harbor Dam, Washington (state record)
Chicago Bears (NFL) beat Montreal Alouettes (CFL) 34-16 in a pre-season exhibition at Molson Stadium in Montreal
The first quasar, 3C 273, is located through radio observations
UN command forbids flights to Congo's breakaway Katangese airports
Britain, USA, and USSR sign the Nuclear Test Ban Treaty
Beatles record a cover of Little Willie John's "Leave My Kitten Alone"; not released until 1995's "Anthology 1"
Congo rebellion: Christopher Gbenye and Pierre Mulele conquer Stanleyville
The US begins bombing North Vietnam in response to the real and imagined attacks on US Navy ships in the Gulf of Tonkin
33rd NFL Chicago College All-Star Game at Soldier Field: Green Bay 38, All-Stars 0 (72,000 attendees)
The Beatles release the single "Yellow Submarine" with "Eleanor Rigby" in the UK
The Beatles release their "Revolver" album in the UK
USSR conducts a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Bobby Gentry releases her hit "Ode to Billie Joe"
British pirate radio station Radio 355 ceases broadcasting
First exhibition after AFL/NFL merger, Denver Broncos (AFL) surprise Detroit Lions (NFL), 13-7 at the University of Denver
WMCV (now WZTV) TV channel 17 in Nashville, TN (IND) broadcasts for the first time
Mariner 7 flies past Mars
The UVF plants its first bomb in the Republic of Ireland, damaging the RTÉ Television Centre in Dublin
Robert Morley's "How the Other Half Loves" premieres in London
US Black Panther leader Huey Newton is freed from jail after two trials end in hung juries, and the district attorney decides against a fourth trial
The British Parliament debate the security situation in Northern Ireland
WNPE TV channel 16 in Watertown, NY (PBS) begins broadcasting
Detroit Tigers shortstop Ed Brinkman commits an error, ending a record streak of 72 games and 331 total chances without a misplay
The Moody Blues reissue their 1967 single "Nights in White Satin," and it reaches #2 on the US charts, higher than the initial release
Arab terrorists open fire at Athens airport, killing 3 and injuring 55
Fifteen-year-old Australian swimmer Stephen Holland smashes Mike Burton's 1,500 m world record by a massive 14.78 seconds (15:37.8) in Brisbane
USSR launches Mars 6
Joan Jett forms her rock group, The Runaways, in the United States
Philadelphia Phillies set an MLB record when the first eight batters get hits off Bill Bonham and beat the Cubs 13-5
New Orleans Saints beat Philadelphia Eagles 14-7 in an NFL exhibition at Mexico City's Olympic Stadium
The first all-seater stadium opens in Aberdeen, Scotland; Aberdeen and London club Tottenham Hotspur play a friendly to inaugurate Pittodrie Stadium
Government of Mauritania signs peace treaty with Polisario
Italian government of Cossiga is sworn in
Varetta Shankle (Miss) is crowned the 12th Miss Black America
Montreal Expos manager Dick Williams wins his 1,000th career game in an 11-5 victory against the Mets, for whom Doug Flynn hits an MLB record-tying three triples
US conducts a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Stephanie Hix of Alabama is crowned Miss National Teenager
US conducts a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
American rock singer David Crosby sentenced to 5 years in Texas state prison on drug and weapons convictions
American super-hurdler Edwin Moses wins the 400 m hurdles gold medal at the Los Angeles Olympics, his 105th consecutive race victory
Evelyn Ashford runs a 100 m record time of 10.97 to beat American teammate Alice Brown in the Los Angeles Olympic Games final
The rowing competition at the Los Angeles Olympics concludes with Romania dominating the women's section, winning 5 of 6 gold medals, while the US wins the eights from Romania
Toronto designated hitter Cliff Johnson hits an MLB record 19th career pinch-hit HR in the Blue Jays' 4-3 win over the Baltimore Orioles
Establishment of the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame is announced
It is revealed that Andrew Wyeth of Chadds Ford, Pennsylvania, secretly created 240 drawings and paintings of his neighbor Helga Testorf
The US Senate approves funding for the Strategic Defense Initiative, commonly known as "Star Wars"
American politician Mario Biaggi (Rep-D-NY) is convicted of racketeering and resigns his seat
Thoroughbred trainer Dale Baird wins his 5,000th career race with Stuffed Johnnie at Mountaineer Park, becoming the second to reach the milestone behind Jack Van Berg
First-ever tie in harness racing's Hambletonian as Park Avenue Joe and Probe tie the third and final heat; Park Avenue Joe wins the series on a countback
General elections are held in Nicaragua; Sandinista Front wins a majority
NASA's Venus probe Magellan launches from the Space Shuttle, the first to do so
Sergei Bubka of the Soviet Union breaks his own world pole vault record (6.10 m) in Malmö, Sweden
Cuba wins baseball's inaugural gold medal at the Olympic Games in Barcelona, beating Chinese Taipei 11-1
Republican Guard kills 64 civilians at Gninguilim marketplace in Chad
Antonov-12 crashes in Boda, eastern Siberia, killing 47 people
British rocker Billy Idol admitted to the hospital after drug overdose in Los Angeles, California
Kansas City Chiefs rout Dallas Cowboys 32-6 in an NFL preseason exhibition game in Monterrey, Mexico
Korean Air 747 with 331 aboard crashes in Guam; 29 survive
Ramzi Yousef, the Pakistani mastermind of the 1993 World Trade Center bombing, goes on trial in New York; he is eventually found guilty and sentenced to life imprisonment plus 240 years
Soyuz TM-26 crew of Anatoly Solovyov and Pavel Vinogradov is launched
Moni Maker wins the $500,000 Nat Ray at the Meadowlands in a world record time of 1:52.1 to become harness racing's first $5 million stakes earner
Cleveland Indians tie an MLB record by erasing a 12-run Seattle lead to win 15-14, also preventing the Mariners from reaching a record 117 season wins
A car bomb explodes in the Indonesian capital of Jakarta outside the Marriott Hotel, killing 12 and injuring 150
Discovery of Skull 5, the earliest complete Homo erectus skull aged 1.8 million years, at Dmanisi, Georgia, by archaeologist David Lordkipanidze, site of the first known hominins to leave Africa
NBA announces Las Vegas will host the 2007 All-Star Game, the first time the event will be held in a city without an NBA team
NASA launches its Juno space probe from Cape Canaveral to orbit and study Jupiter
Svalbard Polar Bear Attack: A rogue polar bear attacks and kills a British schoolboy
Gunman opens fire on Sikh temple in Oak Creek, Wisconsin, killing six people before committing suicide
Kurdish rebels kill 19 people in Hakkâri Province, Turkey
Flash floods across Afghanistan and Pakistan kill 160 people
Rain intervenes to end the third cricket Test between Australia and England at Old Trafford; England retains the Ashes after winning the first two Tests
The world's first bovine stem cell lab-grown burger is eaten in London
Fishing boat carrying around 600 migrants sinks off the coast of Libya; 373 people are rescued
Torrential rains and flooding leave at least 178 people dead in India following Cyclone Komen
Battle of Ponza: KingAlfonso Vof Aragon is captured by the Genoese
Gowrie Conspiracy: Ruthven family lures Scottish KingJames VIto Gowrie House, Perth, supposedly to kill him and avenge their father; two Ruthven brothers are killed by the King's supporters

War of 1812:Tecumseh's Native American force of 25 ambushes Thomas Van Horne's 200 US militiamen at Brownstown Creek in Michigan Territory; US forces retreat haphazardly with 18 killed, 12 wounded, and 70 abandoning the battle
The firsttransatlantic telegraph cablelands at Trinity Bay, Newfoundland, spearheaded by Cyrus West Field, but it fails after 3 weeks
Battle of Mobile Bay won by the Union Navy, led by Rear AdmiralDavid Farragutwith the cry "Damn the torpedoes, full speed ahead!"
First meeting of the Russian and Japanese peace commissioners takes place at US PresidentTheodore Roosevelt's home in Oyster Bay, New York
Battle of Liège: first battle of WWI, massive German assault on forts around the Belgian city of Liège led byErich Ludendorff
WWI: Field MarshalLord Kitchenerappointed Secretary of War after British declaration of war on Germany
Last German air raid on England in World War I occurs, with four Zeppelin airships dropping bombs in the Midlands and North East England
Harry Houdinistays in a coffin underwater for 1½ hours before escaping
Clyde Barrowand two associates kill Sheriff C.G. Maxwell and his deputy, Eugene C. Moore, in the first instance of the Barrow gang killing a lawman
American athleteJesse Owenswins the 200 m in a world record time of 20.7 seconds, his third gold medal of the Berlin Olympics
"From Here to Eternity," based on the book by James Jones, directed by Fred Zinnemann and starringBurt Lancaster,Montgomery Clift, andFrank Sinatra, is released (Academy Awards Best Picture 1954)
Nelson Mandelais arrested for incitement and for illegally leaving South Africa
Craig Breedlovesets a world land speed record of 407.45 mph (655.73 km/h) in the Spirit of America at Bonneville Salt Flats, Utah
Protesters throw rocks and bottles atMartin Luther King Jr.during a fair housing protest march at Marquette Park in Chicago
Pirates outfielderWillie Stargellsmashes his first and longest home run ever hit out of Dodger Stadium; the incomparable shot off Alan Foster measures 506 feet
Atlanta Braves pitcherPhil Niekrono-hits the San Diego Padres 9-0, achieving the first no-hitter for the franchise in Atlanta
US PresidentRichard Nixonadmits he withheld information about Watergate break-in
Stevie Wondersigns a 7-year, 7-album contract with a $13 million advance with Motown Records
Philadelphia Phillies first basemanPete Rosecollects a National League record 2,427th career single in a 5-2 home loss against the Pirates
Willie Mays, Warren Giles, andHack Wilsoninducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, NY
US PresidentRonald Reaganfires more than 11,000 striking air traffic controllers
American Joan Benoit wins the first Olympic marathon for women (2:24:52) ahead ofGrete Waitzof Norway at the Los Angeles Games
Giants left-handed pitcherSteve Carltonbecomes the second player to reach the 4,000 strikeout milestone when he strikes out center fielder Eric Davis in an 11-5 loss to the Reds
Ingrid Kristiansenof Norway sets a 5,000 m world record in 14:37.33 in Stockholm, Sweden

The city of Knin, a significant Serb stronghold, is captured by Croatian forces during Operation Storm. The date is celebrated as the day of victory (Victory and Homeland Thanksgiving Day) in Croatia.
The American spin-off "Whose Line Is It Anyway?", starring Ryan Stiles, Colin Mochrie, andWayne Brady, debuts on ABC
St. Louis sluggerMark McGwirebecomes the 16th MLB player to reach the 500 home run milestone as the Cardinals lose 10-3 to San Diego at Busch Stadium
St. Louis Cardinals 1st basemanAlbert Pujolsbecomes the first player to connect for 30 home runs in each of his first five MLB seasons
Warren Moon becomes the first Black quarterback to be inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in Canton, Ohio, joined by Troy Aikman, John Madden, Rayfield Wright, Harry Carson, andReggie White
New York Mets left-handerTom Glavinebecomes the 23rd pitcher in MLB history to reach the 300-win milestone in an 8-3 victory over the Cubs at Wrigley Field in Chicago

Group led by Hall of Fame pitcherNolan Ryanand Pittsburgh attorney Chuck Greenberg is confirmed as the winning bidder in a court-ordered auction of the Texas Rangers MLB franchise, valued at $593 million
Jamaican sprint superstarUsain Boltwins the 100 m at the London Olympics in 9.63 seconds, breaking the record he set in the 2008 Olympics
MLB bans New York Yankees infielderAlex Rodriguezfor 211 games due to the Biogenesis Laboratories scandal
Samantha Powerbecomes the 28th United States Ambassador to the United Nations
President Obamasigns the Iron Dome Bill, providing $225 million in additional funding for Israel's Iron Dome defense
Scottish resistance leaderWilliam Wallaceis captured by the English near Glasgow and transported to London for trial and execution
Battle of Petrovaradin [Peterwardein]: Habsburgs underEugene of Savoydefeat the Ottomans in a decisive victory
Cornerstone for the Statue of Liberty is laid on Bedloe's Island, New York City
The Norwegian Bratvaag expedition finds the remains, diaries and negative images ofS. A. Andrée’s ill-fated balloon polar expedition of 1897 on White Island in the Svalbard archipelago
Four officers of the Los Angeles Police Department, previously acquitted on charges of beatingRodney King, are indicted on federal civil rights charges
King Ladislaus IV ("the Cuman"), Árpád dynasty King of Hungary and Croatia (1272-90), born in Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1290)
Edmund of Woodstock, 1st Earl of Kent, son of King Edward I, beheaded for plotting against King Edward III, born in Woodstock, Oxfordshire (d. 1330)
Guillaume Dufay, Franco-Flemish Renaissance composer and theorist, born in Beersel, Burgundian Netherlands (d. 1474) [date disputed]
Alexander Jagiellon, Grand Duke of Lithuania (1492-1506) and King of Poland (1501-6), born in Kraków, Poland (d. 1506)
Joseph Justus Scaliger, French-Dutch scholar (revolutionised ideas about ancient chronology), born in Agen, France (d. 1609)
Charles d'Albert, duc of Luynes, French courtier, favourite of Louis XIII and Constable of France, born in Longueville, France (d. 1621)
John Eliot, English Puritan missionary "Apostle to Indians,", whose Algonquian translation of the bible was the first bible published in North America, baptized in Widford, England (d. 1690)
Philipp Friedrich Boddecker, German composer and court organist, born in Hagenau, Alsace. Holy Roman Empire (d. 1683)
Antonio Cesti, Italian composer, singer and musician, baptized in Arezzo, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1669)
John Hathorne, American merchant and magistrate (judge during Salem witch trials, 1692), born in Salem, Massachusetts Bay Colony (d. 1717)
James Anderson, Scottish historian, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1728)
Vitus Bering, Danish explorer of Bering Strait and coast of Alaska, born in Horsens, Denmark (d. 1741)
Leonardo Leo, Italian composer, born in San Vito degli Schiavi, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1744)
Johan Lulofs, Dutch physicist, philosopher and astronomer, born in Zutphen, Netherlands (d. 1758)
Thomas Lynch, American lawyer, signer of the US Declaration of Independence, born in Winyah, South Carolina (d. 1779)
Go-Momozono [Hidehito], 118th Emperor of Japan (1771-9), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 1779)
Friedrich August Kummer, German composer and musician, born in Meiningen, Duchy of Saxe-Meiningen, House of Wettin (d. 1879)
Niels H. Abel, Norwegian mathematician (Abel's Comparisons), born in Nedstrand, Norway (d. 1829)
Ambroise Thomas, French opera composer (Mignon), and music professor, born in Metz, France (d. 1896)
Carnot Posey, American Confederate brigadier general, born in Wilkinson County, Mississippi (d. 1863)
Ivar Aasen, Norwegian linguist and poet, born in Ørsten, Norway (d. 1896)
Edward John Eyre, British explorer and Governor of Jamaica, born in Whipsnade, England (d. 1901)
Johann Georg Herzog, German composer and organist, born in Hummendorf, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1909)
Deodoro da Fonseca, Brazilian military officer and the 1st President of Brazil (1889-91), born in Vila Madalena, Brazil (d. 1892)
Giovanni Rossi, Italian composer, born in Borgo San Donnino, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (d. 1886)
Louisa of the Netherlands, Dutch queen consort of Sweden and Norway and princess of the Netherlands, born in the Hague (d. 1871)
Heinrich Stiehl, German organist and composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Lübeck (d. 1886)
Milo Smith Hascall, American banker, real estate executive, and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Le Roy, New York (d. 1904)
Carola of Vasa, Queen consort of Saxony, born in Vienna (d. 1907)
James Scott Skinner, Scottish composer and dancing master, born in Arbeadie, Scotland (d. 1927)
Anton Simon, Russian composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1916)
Joseph Merrick, "The Elephant Man", born in Leicester, England (d. 1890)
Resurreccion Maria de Azkue, Basque priest, composer and first head of the Euskaltzaindia, born in Lekeitio, Spain (d. 1951)

Alfred Holy, Portuguese composer and harpist (German Opera - Prague, 1885–96); Berlin Royal Opera, 1896–1903, Vienna Court Opera, 1903–13; Boston Symphony, 1913-28), born in Oporto, Portugal (d. 1948)
Carl Dietrich Harries, German chemist (developed synthetic rubber), born in Luckenwalde, Germany (d. 1923)
Harry Trott, Australian cricket all-rounder (24 Tests, 1 x 100, 4 x 50, HS 143, 29 wickets; Victoria CA), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1917)
Jacob Ruppert Jr., American Baseball HOF executive (owner New York Yankees 1915-39; signed Babe Ruth; built Yankee Stadium), born in New York City (d. 1939)
Oskar Merikanto [Ala-Kanto], Finnish pianist, organist, and composer, born in Helsinki, Grand Duchy of Finland (d. 1924)
Oswaldo Cruz, Brazilian physician and epidemiologist, born in São Luis do Paraitinga, Brazil (d. 1917)
Horace Rawlins, English golfer (US Open 1895), born in Shanklin, Isle of Wight (d. 1935)
Mary Ritter Beard, American historian (Woman as a Force in History), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1958)
Tom Thomson, Canadian painter, born in Claremont, Ontario (d. 1917)
Gertrude E Durden Rush, American composer, and playwright (Black Girls Burden), born in Navasota, Texas (d. 1962)
Joe English, Irish-Flemish draftsman, painter, and soldier (designed WWI gravestones), born in Bruges, Belgium (d. 1918)
Frank Nelson, English politician and civil servant (head of the Special Operations Executive, 1940-42), born in Gloucestershire (d. 1966)
Óscar Esplá Spanish composer, born in Alicante, Spain (d. 1976)
Reginald Owen, British actor (Mary Poppins, Sherlock Holmes), born in Wheathampstead, Hertfordshire, England (d. 1972)
Victor Francen, Belgian actor (J'Acusse, San Antonio), born in Tienen, Flanders, Belgium (d. 1977)
Conrad Aiken, American Pulitzer poet and writer (Ushant/Conversation), born in Savannah, Georgia (d. 1973)
Erich Kleiber, Austrian-Argentine conductor (Teatro Colón, 1937-49; NBC Symphony, 1947-48), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1956)
Hans Gál, Austrian-British composer (Die heilige Ente (The Sacred Duck)), born in Brunn am Gebirge, Austria (d. 1987)
Naum Gabo [Pevsner], Russian-American sculptor (Stillife Flower), born in Bryans, Russian Empire (d. 1977)
Donald Kerr, American actor (Carnival Lady, Devil Bat), born in Eagle Grove, Iowa (d. 1977)
Vera Kholodnaya (née Levchenko;, Russian silent film actress (Song of Triumphant Love (Pesn Torzhestvuyushchey Lyubvi)), born in Poltava, Russian Empire (d. 1919)
Aksel Larsen, Danish politician, founder of the Socialist People's Party, born in Brændekilde, Denmark (d. 1972)
Bodi Rapp [Johanna Goetmakers], Dutch mezzo-soprano and teacher, born in Almelo, Netherlands (d. 1977)
Claude Autant-Lara, French film director (Four Bags Full, Rouge et noir), born in Luzarches, Val-d'Oise, France (d. 2000)
Juan Carlos Paz, Argentine composer, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1972)
Irene Rice Pereira, American abstract artist, born in Chelsea, Massachusetts (d. 1971)
Kenneth Thimann, English American botanist (Balzan Prize), born in Ashford, England (d. 1997)
Francis Walder [Francois Waldburger], Belgian author, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1997)
John Huston, American director and writer (African Queen, Chinatown), born in Nevada, Missouri (d. 1987)
Wassely Leontief, Russian American economist (Nobel 1973), born in Munich, Germany (d. 1999)
Harold Holt, Australian politician, Prime Minister of Australia (1966-67); supported US in Vietnam, disappeared swimming, presumed drowned while PM, born in Stanmore, New South Wales (d. 1967)
Ernestine "Tiny" Davis, American jazz vocalist and trumpeter (International Sweethearts of Rhythm), born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1994)
Bruno Coquatrix, French songwriter, music impresario (Olympia Hall, Paris), and politician, born in Ronchin, France (d. 1979)
Jacquetta Hawkes, English archaeologist, author, and first woman to study an Archaeology & Anthropology degree at the University of Cambridge, born in Cambridge, England (d. 1996)
Nan Gindele, US javelin thrower (World Record 1932-38), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1992)
[Johan] Erik Lindegren, Swedish poet/librettist (Aniara), born in Luleå, Sweden (d. 1968)
Robert Taylor, American actor (Waterloo Bridge, Quo Vadis, Death Valley Days), born in Filley, Nebraska (d. 1969)
Abbé Pierre [Henri Grouès], French Catholic priest and founder of the Emmaus movement (1949), born in Lyon, France (d. 2007)[1]
Anita Colby, American model, author and actress (Mary of Scotland, Cover Girl, Pepsi Cola Playhouse), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1992)
David Brian, American actor (Accused of Murder, Dawn at Sorocco), born in New York City (d. 1993)
Parley Baer, American radio, film and television character actor (Gunsmoke; The Andy Griffith Show - "Mayor Stoner"), born in Salt Lake City Utah (d. 2002)
Stjepan Sulek, Croatian violinist and composer (Coriolan), born in Zagreb, Croatia (d. 1986)
Jan Bos, Dutch resistance fighter, born in Musselkanaal, Netherlands (d. 1945)
Betty Oliphant, British-Canadian ballerina, co-founder of National Ballet School of Canada, born in London, England (d. 2004)
Tom Drake [Alfred Sinclair Alderdice], American actor (Meet Me In St. Louis; Warlock), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1982)
Selma Diamond, Canadian-American comedy writer (Your Show Of Shows), and actress (Night Court, 1984-85 - "Selma"), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1985)
Sonny Montgomery, American politician (Rep-D-Mississippi, 1967-97), born in Meridian, Mississippi (d. 2006)
Nazar Mohammad, Pakistani cricket batsman (5 Tests, 1 x 100 Pakistan's 1st centurion), born in Lahore, Pakistan (d. 1996)
L. Tom Perry, American religious leader (LDS apostle), born in Logan, Utah (d. 2015)
Devan Nair, Singaporean politician, President of Singapore (1981-85), born in Melaka (d. 2005)
Richard Kleindienst, American lawyer and civil servant (Attorney General during Watergate scandal, 1972)-73, born in Winslow, Arizona (d. 2000)
Billie Hayes [Brosch], American stage, film, and television actress Li'l Abner - "Mammy Yokum"; Witchiepoo), born in town of DuQuoin, Illinois (d. 2021)
Cor Witschge, Dutch actor (Pipo the Clown), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1991)
Betsy Jolas, French-American composer (Les Belles Années (The Good Years), Le Cyclope), born in Paris, France
Jeri Southern [Genevieve Hering], American jazz singer ("You Better Go Now"; "Joey"), and pianist, born in Royal, Nebraska (d. 1991)
Gunnar Bucht, Swedish composer, pedagogue, and musicologist, born in Stocksund, Sweden
Peter Easterby, English racehorse trainer (British jump racing Champion Trainer 1978/79, 1979/80, 1980/81), born at Knayton, England (d. 2025)
Joan Weldon, American actress (So This is Love, Them), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2021)
Michal Kováč, 1st President of Slovakia (1993-98), born in Ľubiša, Czechoslovakia (d. 2016)
Billy Bingham, Irish soccer forward (56 caps Northern Ireland; Sunderland, Luton Town, Everton) and manager (Northern Ireland, Greece, Everton), born in Belfast, Northern Ireland (d. 2022)
